lists.title: Greek A1 Grammar Path
lists.description: A1 Modern Greek in communicative order: pronouns, the three genders with their articles, είμαι and έχω, the present tense, the accusative object articles, negation with δεν, υπάρχει, questions, and adjective agreement.

points.title: Subject pronouns (εγώ, εσύ, αυτός…)
points.function: Say who you are talking about — when it needs saying
points.explanation: Εγώ (I), εσύ (you), αυτός/αυτή/αυτό (he/she/it), εμείς (we), εσείς (you all — also the polite singular), αυτοί/αυτές/αυτά (they). The verb ending names the person, so Greek drops the pronoun except for emphasis: μιλάω ελληνικά.
points.culture_note: Εσείς is both plural and polite — like French vous. Greeks switch to εσύ quickly among peers.

references.title: Portal for the Greek Language (Centre for the Greek Language)

drills.translation: I am from Athens.
You are my friend.
She is my sister.
We are students.
They are from Spain.
You (all) are very kind.
He is our teacher.
It is difficult.
I live in Thessaloniki.
You speak very fast.
He works at the bank.
She lives in the centre.
It is very expensive.
We have a house in the village.
Do you (all) speak English?
They are playing in the garden.

points.title: Gender and number of nouns
points.function: Name people and things, one or many
points.explanation:
Three genders, visible in the endings: masculine -ος (ο δρόμος), feminine -α/-η (η μέρα, η αγάπη), neuter -ο/-ι (το βιβλίο, το παιδί).

Plurals: -ος → -οι, -α/-η → -ες, -ο → -α, -ι → -ιά (βιβλίο → βιβλία, παιδί → παιδιά).

references.title: Portal for the Greek Language (Centre for the Greek Language)

drills.translation: I have two books.
The children are playing outside.
I have three sisters.
The streets are nice.
I have two cats at home.
The cars are new.
The coffees are hot.

points.title: Definite articles (ο / η / το)
points.function: Point to a specific thing
points.explanation: Ο for masculine (ο δρόμος), η for feminine (η μέρα), το for neuter (το βιβλίο); plurals οι, οι, τα. Greek uses the article even before names: η Μαρία, ο Γιώργος.

references.title: Portal for the Greek Language (Centre for the Greek Language)

drills.translation: The street is big.
The day is beautiful.
The book is new.
The coffee is hot.
The door is open.
The child is sleeping.

points.title: Indefinite articles (ένας / μία / ένα)
points.function: Mention something for the first time
points.explanation: Ένας for masculine (ένας κήπος), μία/μια for feminine (μία μέρα), ένα for neuter (ένα σπίτι). They double as the number 'one' — no plural form exists.

references.title: Portal for the Greek Language (Centre for the Greek Language)

drills.translation: It is a beautiful day.
He is a good friend.
It is a big house.
I have an idea.
It is a good book.
It is a beautiful garden.

points.title: Είμαι — present (είμαι, είσαι, είναι…)
points.function: Say who or what someone is
points.explanation: Είμαι (I am), είσαι, είναι, είμαστε, είστε, είναι — note είναι serves he/she/it AND they. Professions take no article: είναι δασκάλα.

references.title: Portal for the Greek Language (Centre for the Greek Language)

drills.translation: I am a student.
You are very tall.
She is a teacher.
We are friends.
Are you (all) Greek?
They are at home.
I am from Crete.
You are a good friend.
My father is a doctor.
We are ready.
Are you (all) from Cyprus?
The children are in the garden.

points.title: Έχω — present (έχω, έχεις, έχει…)
points.function: Say what you have — and who's right
points.explanation:
Έχω (to have): έχω, έχεις, έχει, έχουμε, έχετε, έχουν.

Being right is HAD: έχεις δίκιο. But age uses 'to be': είμαι είκοσι χρονών — Greek sides with English there.

references.title: Portal for the Greek Language (Centre for the Greek Language)

drills.translation: I have a question.
Do you have a cat?
She has a bicycle.
We have time.
You (all) are right.
They have a house by the sea.
I have two sisters.
Do you have time today?
He has many books.
We have a big table.
Do you (all) have children?
They have two cats.

points.title: Present tense of -ω verbs
points.function: Talk about what people do
points.explanation:
The core pattern: μένω, μένεις, μένει, μένουμε, μένετε, μένουν (to live/stay).

The Greek present covers both English presents: μένω = I live AND I am living.

references.title: Portal for the Greek Language (Centre for the Greek Language)

drills.translation: I live in Athens.
She works in an office.
We are learning Greek.
They play football.
Do you listen to music?
I am reading a book.
Do you (all) live in Athens?
Are you writing a letter?
He reads a newspaper.
We are drinking a coffee.
Do you (all) listen to Greek music?
They run in the park.

points.title: The accusative object (τον / την / το)
points.function: Mark what the action lands on
points.explanation:
Objects take the accusative — mostly visible in the article: ο → τον, η → την, το stays το.

Masculine nouns also drop the final -ς: ο δρόμος → βλέπω τον δρόμο.

Keep the ν on τον for masculine so it never sounds neuter; feminine την keeps ν only before vowels and hard stops.

references.title: Portal for the Greek Language (Centre for the Greek Language)

drills.translation: I see the street.
I see the door.
drills.hint: feminine object — ν stays before π

drills.translation: I hear the child.
I love the sea.
drills.hint: feminine — the ν drops before θ

drills.translation: I am reading the book.
I ask the teacher.

points.title: Negation with δεν
points.function: Say what is not so
points.explanation: Δεν goes directly before the verb: δεν μιλάω αγγλικά, δεν έχω χρόνο. (Commands use μην instead — that comes later.)

references.title: Portal for the Greek Language (Centre for the Greek Language)

drills.translation: I don't speak English.
He doesn't work on Sundays.
We are not tired.
She doesn't eat meat.
Aren't you eating?
You (all) don't dance?

points.title: Υπάρχει / υπάρχουν — there is, there are
points.function: Say what exists or is available
points.explanation: Υπάρχει for one thing (υπάρχει ένα βιβλίο), υπάρχουν for several (υπάρχουν δύο καφετέριες). Negative: δεν υπάρχει γάλα.

references.title: Portal for the Greek Language (Centre for the Greek Language)

drills.translation: There is a book on the table.
There are two cafés on the street.
There is no milk.
There are many people here.
Is there a park nearby?
There are no problems.

points.title: Question words (πού, τι, πότε…)
points.function: Ask about places, people, time, and reasons
points.explanation: Πού (where), τι (what), πότε (when), ποιος (who), πώς (how), γιατί (why). Greek's question mark is the semicolon: Πού μένεις;
points.culture_note: The Greek question mark ; trips up every learner once. The Greek semicolon, for the record, is a raised dot (·).

references.title: Portal for the Greek Language (Centre for the Greek Language)

drills.translation: Where do you live?
What is your name?
When is your birthday?
What is this?
Who is your teacher?
Why are you crying?

points.title: Adjective agreement (καλός / καλή / καλό)
points.function: Describe people and things correctly
points.explanation:
Adjectives take the noun's gender, number, and case: καλός, καλή, καλό (good m/f/n), plurals καλοί, καλές, καλά.

They usually come BEFORE the noun, article first: ο καλός φίλος.

references.title: Portal for the Greek Language (Centre for the Greek Language)

drills.translation: The house is big.
The books are new.
My sister is very kind.
The coffee is hot.
The flowers are beautiful.
The film is interesting.
The gardens are beautiful.
The days are long.
The sky is clear.
The food is delicious.
The streets are empty.
The doors are closed.

points.title: The past — aorist (αόριστος)
points.function: Talk about completed past actions
points.explanation:
The aorist is the plain past ('I wrote').

It's built on the verb's perfective stem with past endings and, when needed, a stressed augment ε- at the front: γράφω → έγραψα, έγραψες, έγραψε, γράψαμε, γράψατε, έγραψαν.

The stress falls on the third syllable from the end, which is why singular forms take the augment but γράψαμε/γράψατε don't need it.

references.title: Portal for the Greek Language (Centre for the Greek Language)

drills.sentence: Χθες {{answer}} ένα γράμμα στη φίλη μου.
drills.translation: Yesterday I wrote a letter to my friend.
drills.transliteration: Chthes ___ éna grámma sti fíli mu.

drills.sentence: Το πρωί {{answer}} τον καφέ μου γρήγορα.
drills.translation: In the morning I drank my coffee quickly.
drills.transliteration: To proí ___ ton kafé mu grígora.

drills.translation: Did you study the lesson last night?
When did you get home?

drills.sentence: Ο Γιώργος {{answer}} το τρένο το πρωί.
drills.translation: George missed the train in the morning.
drills.transliteration: O Yiórgos ___ to tréno to proí.

drills.translation: Maria sang a beautiful song.
In the summer we went to Crete.
Yesterday we ate together at the restaurant.
What time did you (pl.) arrive at the party?
Did you (pl.) prepare your suitcases?

drills.sentence: Τα παιδιά {{answer}} στην αυλή όλο το απόγευμα.
drills.translation: The children played in the yard all afternoon.
drills.transliteration: Ta pediá ___ stin avlí ólo to apóyevma.

drills.translation: The students studied for the exams.

points.title: The genitive case (γενική)
points.function: Show possession and relationships
points.explanation:
The genitive marks 'of' — possession, belonging, quantity.

The article changes: ο→του, η→της, το→του, οι/τα→των, and the noun ending shifts (ο φίλος → του φίλου, η μητέρα → της μητέρας, το παιδί → του παιδιού).

The possessor follows the thing possessed: το βιβλίο του φοιτητή 'the student's book'.

references.title: Portal for the Greek Language (Centre for the Greek Language)

drills.sentence: Αυτό είναι το αυτοκίνητο {{answer}} πατέρα μου.
drills.translation: This is my father's car.
drills.transliteration: Aftó íne to aftokínito ___ patéra mu.

drills.translation: The colour of the sea is blue.
The door of the house is open.
The children's toys are here.
My sister's friend came.
The beginning of the lesson is difficult.

points.title: Plural of nouns (οι φίλοι, οι πόρτες, τα βιβλία)
points.function: Make nouns plural
points.explanation:
Plurals depend on gender: masculine -ος → -οι (ο φίλος → οι φίλοι), feminine -α/-η → -ες (η πόρτα → οι πόρτες, η νίκη → οι νίκες), neuter -ο → -α (το βιβλίο → τα βιβλία) and -ι → -ια (το παιδί → τα παιδιά).

The article becomes οι (masc/fem) or τα (neut).

references.title: Portal for the Greek Language (Centre for the Greek Language)

drills.translation: My friends came to the party.
The doors of the house are closed.
The books are on the table.
The children are playing on the beach.
The teachers teach well.
I bought three chairs for the house.

points.title: The imperfect (παρατατικός)
points.function: Describe ongoing or habitual past actions
points.explanation:
The imperfect describes a past action that was ongoing, repeated, or a background state — English 'was ...-ing' or 'used to'.

It's built on the present (imperfective) stem with past endings: γράφω → έγραφα 'I was writing', έτρωγα 'I used to eat'.

Contrast the aorist έγραψα (one completed act) with the imperfect έγραφα (ongoing).

references.title: Portal for the Greek Language (Centre for the Greek Language)

drills.translation: Every summer we used to go to the village.
When I was little, I used to read a lot.
What were you doing when I called you?

drills.sentence: Ο παππούς μου {{answer}} ιστορίες κάθε βράδυ.
drills.translation: My grandfather used to tell stories every night.
drills.transliteration: O pappús mu ___ istoríes káthe vrádi.

drills.sentence: Τα παιδιά {{answer}} στον κήπο όλη μέρα.
drills.translation: The children were playing in the garden all day.
drills.transliteration: Ta pediá ___ ston kípo óli méra.

drills.translation: We used to live in a small apartment.

points.title: The future with θα
points.function: Talk about what will happen
points.explanation: The future is simply θα + verb. θα + the perfective (aorist) stem is a single future act: θα γράψω 'I will write'; θα + the present stem is a continuous or habitual future: θα γράφω 'I will be writing'. Negate with δεν θα.

references.title: Portal for the Greek Language (Centre for the Greek Language)

drills.translation: Tomorrow I will go to work early.
In the evening we will watch a film.
Will you come with us on the trip?

drills.sentence: Η Άννα θα {{answer}} το δώρο αύριο.
drills.translation: Anna will buy the gift tomorrow.
drills.transliteration: I Ánna tha ___ to dóro ávrio.

drills.sentence: Του χρόνου θα {{answer}} ελληνικά κάθε μέρα.
drills.translation: Next year I will be learning Greek every day.
drills.transliteration: Tu chrónu tha ___ elliniká káthe méra.

drills.sentence: Οι φίλοι μου {{answer}} είναι στο πάρτι.
drills.translation: My friends will be at the party.
drills.transliteration: I fíli mu ___ íne sto párti.

points.title: Object pronouns (τον, την, το…)
points.function: Replace the object with a short pronoun
points.explanation:
A known object becomes a weak pronoun placed BEFORE the verb: τον (him), την (her), το (it), τους/τις/τα (them), με (me), σε (you), μας/σας (us/you).

Τον βλέπω 'I see him'. In commands it follows the verb (δώσε μου).

These are accusative forms — the indirect object uses genitive (μου, σου, του…).

references.title: Portal for the Greek Language (Centre for the Greek Language)

drills.sentence: Ξέρω τον Γιώργο· {{answer}} βλέπω κάθε μέρα.
drills.translation: I know George; I see him every day.
drills.transliteration: Xéro ton Yiórgo; ___ vlépo káthe méra.

drills.sentence: Πού είναι η Μαρία; Δεν {{answer}} βρίσκω.
drills.translation: Where is Maria? I can't find her.
drills.transliteration: Pú íne i María? Den ___ vrísko.

drills.translation: Nice book! I'm reading it now.
The keys? I left them on the table.
If you come, I'll wait for you at the stop.
We're here; can you hear us?

points.title: Comparative & superlative (πιο…, ο πιο…)
points.function: Compare things
points.explanation:
The comparative puts πιο before the adjective + από 'than': πιο μεγάλος από 'bigger than'. The superlative makes it definite: ο πιο μεγάλος 'the biggest'.

Many adjectives also have a one-word form in -τερος/-τατος (μεγαλύτερος), but πιο works everywhere.

references.title: Portal for the Greek Language (Centre for the Greek Language)

drills.sentence: Η Αθήνα είναι {{answer}} μεγάλη από τη Θεσσαλονίκη.
drills.translation: Athens is bigger than Thessaloniki.
drills.transliteration: I Athína íne ___ megáli apó ti Thessaloníki.

drills.sentence: Αυτός ο δρόμος είναι ο {{answer}} σύντομος.
drills.translation: This road is the shortest.
drills.transliteration: Aftós o drómos íne o ___ síndomos.

drills.sentence: Τα ελληνικά είναι {{answer}} δύσκολα από τα ισπανικά για μένα.
drills.translation: Greek is harder than Spanish for me.
drills.transliteration: Ta elliniká íne ___ dískola apó ta ispaniká yia ména.

drills.sentence: Είναι ο {{answer}} έξυπνος μαθητής της τάξης.
drills.translation: He is the smartest student in the class.
drills.transliteration: Íne o ___ éxipnos mathitís tis táxis.

drills.sentence: Το καλοκαίρι οι μέρες είναι {{answer}} μεγάλες.
drills.translation: In summer the days are longer.
drills.transliteration: To kalokéri i méres íne ___ megáles.

drills.sentence: Η υγεία είναι {{answer}} σημαντική από τα χρήματα.
drills.translation: Health is more important than money.
drills.transliteration: I iyía íne ___ simandikí apó ta chrímata.

points.title: Prepositions (σε, από, με, για…)
points.function: Say where, from, with, and for
points.explanation: Modern Greek prepositions take the accusative: σε 'in/to/at' (often fused with the article: στο, στη), από 'from', με 'with', για 'for/about', χωρίς 'without', προς 'towards'. σε + accusative covers both location and motion.

references.title: Portal for the Greek Language (Centre for the Greek Language)

drills.translation: I live in a small town.
I came from Athens yesterday.
I'm going to the cinema with my friends.

drills.sentence: Αγόρασα ένα δώρο {{answer}} τη μητέρα μου.
drills.translation: I bought a gift for my mother.
drills.transliteration: Agórasa éna dóro ___ ti mitéra mu.

drills.translation: I can't live without coffee.
We walked towards the beach.

points.title: Possessives (το βιβλίο μου…)
points.function: Say whose something is
points.explanation:
Possession is shown by a weak genitive pronoun placed AFTER the noun: μου (my), σου (your), του/της (his/her), μας (our), σας (your), τους (their).

Το βιβλίο μου 'my book', το σπίτι τους 'their house'. The noun keeps its article: η μητέρα μου.

references.title: Portal for the Greek Language (Centre for the Greek Language)

drills.translation: My car is new.
Where is your phone?
Her sister lives in Larissa.
His dog is very friendly.
Our neighbours are very kind.

drills.sentence: Το σπίτι {{answer}} είναι κοντά στη θάλασσα.
drills.translation: Their house is near the sea.
drills.transliteration: To spíti ___ íne kondá sti thálassa.

points.title: 'want / can / must' + να
points.function: Say what you want, can, or must do
points.explanation:
Greek has no infinitive — after θέλω 'want', μπορώ 'can', πρέπει 'must', you use να + a verb agreeing with the subject: θέλω να φύγω 'I want to leave', πρέπει να διαβάσεις 'you must study', μπορεί να βρέξει 'it may rain'.

Negate the second verb with να μην.

references.title: Portal for the Greek Language (Centre for the Greek Language)

drills.translation: I want to leave early today.
You must study for the exams.
Can you help me?
We want to learn Greek well.
We must be on time.
It may rain tomorrow.

points.title: The subjunctive with να — aspect
points.function: Choose one-off vs repeated with να
points.explanation:
After να (and θα), the choice of stem marks aspect. The aorist (perfective) stem = a single, completed act: θέλω να γράψω ένα γράμμα 'I want to write a letter'.

The present (imperfective) stem = a repeated or ongoing act: θέλω να γράφω κάθε μέρα 'I want to write every day'. Same endings, different stem.

references.title: Portal for the Greek Language (Centre for the Greek Language)

drills.sentence: Θέλω να {{answer}} αυτό το γράμμα τώρα.
drills.translation: I want to write this letter now.
drills.transliteration: Thélo na ___ aftó to grámma tóra.

drills.sentence: Θέλω να {{answer}} στο ημερολόγιό μου κάθε βράδυ.
drills.translation: I want to write in my diary every night.
drills.transliteration: Thélo na ___ sto imerolóyió mu káthe vrádi.

drills.sentence: Πρέπει να {{answer}} το φάρμακο κάθε μέρα.
drills.translation: You must take the medicine every day.
drills.transliteration: Prépi na ___ to fármako káthe méra.

drills.translation: You must take one pill now.

drills.sentence: Μου αρέσει να {{answer}} μουσική όταν μαγειρεύω.
drills.translation: I like to listen to music while I cook.
drills.transliteration: Mu arési na ___ musikí ótan mayirévo.

drills.sentence: Θέλω να {{answer}} αυτό το τραγούδι μία φορά ακόμα.
drills.translation: I want to hear this song one more time.
drills.transliteration: Thélo na ___ aftó to tragúdi mía forá akóma.

points.title: The imperative (γράψε! μην γράφεις!)
points.function: Give direct commands and prohibitions
points.explanation: The positive command has its own form: singular γράψε, plural γράψτε (from the aorist stem for a single act; present stem γράφε for a continuous one). Negative commands don't use it — they use μην + subjunctive: μην γράφεις 'don't write', μην ανησυχείς 'don't worry'.

references.title: Portal for the Greek Language (Centre for the Greek Language)

drills.translation: Give me your phone number, please.
Speak quietly, the baby is sleeping.

drills.sentence: Παιδιά, {{answer}} τα βιβλία σας στη σελίδα δέκα.
drills.translation: Children, open your books to page ten.
drills.transliteration: Pediá, ___ ta vivlía sas sti selída déka.

drills.translation: Be careful when you cross the street.
Don't worry, everything will be fine.
Don't forget to call me.

points.title: Two object pronouns (μου το έδωσε)
points.function: Say 'gave it to me' with both objects
points.explanation: When a verb has both an indirect object (to whom) and a direct one (what), two weak pronouns cluster before the verb in the order genitive + accusative: μου το έδωσε 'he gave it to me', σου τα στέλνω 'I'm sending them to you', της το είπα 'I told it to her'.

references.title: Portal for the Greek Language (Centre for the Greek Language)

drills.sentence: Το βιβλίο; Ο Γιάννης {{answer}} το έδωσε χθες.
drills.translation: The book? John gave it to me yesterday.
drills.transliteration: To vivlío? O Yiánnis ___ to édose chthes.

drills.sentence: Τα κλειδιά; {{answer}} τα έδωσα το πρωί.
drills.translation: The keys? I gave them to you this morning.
drills.transliteration: Ta klidiá? ___ ta édosa to proí.

drills.sentence: Ήξερε την αλήθεια γιατί {{answer}} την είπα.
drills.translation: She knew the truth because I told it to her.
drills.transliteration: Íxere tin alíthia yiatí ___ tin ípa.

drills.sentence: Μου αρέσει το φαγητό· {{answer}} το φτιάχνει η γιαγιά.
drills.translation: I love the food; grandma makes it for me.
drills.transliteration: Mu arési to fayitó; ___ to ftiáchni i yiayiá.

drills.sentence: Αν θέλετε τις φωτογραφίες, {{answer}} τις στέλνω.
drills.translation: If you want the photos, I'll send them to you.
drills.transliteration: An thélete tis fotografíes, ___ tis stélno.

drills.sentence: Το δώρο ήταν έκπληξη· {{answer}} το κράτησα κρυφό.
drills.translation: The gift was a surprise; I kept it secret from them.
drills.transliteration: To dóro ítan ékplixi; ___ to krátisa krifó.

points.title: Relative clauses (που, ο οποίος)
points.function: Join sentences with 'who / which / that'
points.explanation: The all-purpose relative is που, invariable, for people and things alike: ο άντρας που ήρθε 'the man who came', το βιβλίο που διάβασα 'the book that I read'. The formal alternative ο οποίος / η οποία / το οποίο agrees in gender, number, and case, and is used in writing or to avoid ambiguity.

references.title: Portal for the Greek Language (Centre for the Greek Language)

drills.translation: The man who came is a doctor.
The film that we saw was wonderful.
This is the house where I grew up.

drills.sentence: Ο καθηγητής, ο {{answer}} μας δίδαξε, συνταξιοδοτήθηκε.
drills.translation: The professor, who taught us, retired.
drills.transliteration: O kathiyitís, o ___ mas dídaxe, sindaxiodotíthike.

drills.sentence: Τα παιδιά {{answer}} έπαιζαν έξω μπήκαν μέσα.
drills.translation: The children who were playing outside came in.
drills.transliteration: Ta pediá ___ épezan éxo bíkan mésa.

drills.sentence: Η εταιρεία για την {{answer}} δουλεύω είναι μεγάλη.
drills.translation: The company I work for is large.
drills.transliteration: I etería yia tin ___ dulévo íne megáli.

points.title: The mediopassive present (-ομαι)
points.function: Say what happens to the subject, or reflexively
points.explanation:
A second set of endings, -ομαι, -εσαι, -εται, -όμαστε, -εστε, -ονται, makes verbs passive, reflexive, or 'deponent' (mediopassive in form, active in meaning): πλένομαι 'I wash (myself)', ονομάζομαι 'I am named', έρχομαι 'I come', χρειάζομαι 'I need'.

Many common verbs exist only in this set.

references.title: Portal for the Greek Language (Centre for the Greek Language)

drills.sentence: Κάθε πρωί {{answer}} και ετοιμάζομαι για τη δουλειά.
drills.translation: Every morning I wash and get ready for work.
drills.transliteration: Káthe proí ___ ke etimázome yia ti duliá.

drills.translation: What's your name? — My name is Eleni.

drills.sentence: Ο φίλος μου {{answer}} από τη Θεσσαλονίκη απόψε.
drills.translation: My friend is coming from Thessaloniki tonight.
drills.transliteration: O fílos mu ___ apó ti Thessaloníki apópse.

drills.translation: I need a little help with the suitcases.

drills.sentence: Τα μαγαζιά {{answer}} στις εννιά το βράδυ.
drills.translation: The shops are closed at nine in the evening.
drills.transliteration: Ta magaziá ___ stis enniá to vrádi.

drills.translation: We wake up very early on weekdays.

points.title: The perfect (παρακείμενος: έχω γράψει)
points.function: Say what has happened, with present relevance
points.explanation:
The perfect is έχω + an invariable non-finite form (the 'aparémfato', always ending -ει): έχω γράψει 'I have written', έχεις δει 'you have seen', έχουμε φάει 'we have eaten'.

The second part never changes for person or number — only έχω is conjugated. It reports a past act that still matters now.

references.title: Portal for the Greek Language (Centre for the Greek Language)

drills.sentence: {{answer}} διαβάσει αυτό το βιβλίο τρεις φορές.
drills.translation: I have read this book three times.
drills.transliteration: ___ diavási aftó to vivlío tris forés.

drills.translation: Have you ever seen the Acropolis?

drills.sentence: Η Άννα {{answer}} τελειώσει τις σπουδές της.
drills.translation: Anna has finished her studies.
drills.transliteration: I Ánna ___ teliósi tis spudés tis.

drills.translation: We have visited many Greek islands.
I have never tried such food.
My friends have already left.

points.title: Conditionals with αν
points.function: Say 'if… then…' for real possibilities
points.explanation:
For a real or likely condition, αν + present/perfective introduces the 'if' clause, and the result takes θα: αν διαβάσεις, θα περάσεις 'if you study, you'll pass'. αν + present can also give a general truth: αν βρέξει, μένουμε σπίτι 'if it rains, we stay home'.

Negate the condition with αν δεν.

references.title: Portal for the Greek Language (Centre for the Greek Language)

drills.sentence: {{answer}} έχεις χρόνο, έλα να με δεις.
drills.translation: If you have time, come and see me.
drills.transliteration: ___ échis chróno, éla na me dis.

drills.sentence: Αν διαβάσεις πολύ, {{answer}} περάσεις τις εξετάσεις.
drills.translation: If you study a lot, you'll pass the exams.
drills.transliteration: An diavásis polí, ___ perásis tis exetásis.

drills.sentence: Αν {{answer}} αύριο, δεν θα πάμε εκδρομή.
drills.translation: If it rains tomorrow, we won't go on the trip.
drills.transliteration: An ___ ávrio, den tha páme ekdromí.

drills.sentence: {{answer}} δεν βιαστείς, θα χάσεις το λεωφορείο.
drills.translation: If you don't hurry, you'll miss the bus.
drills.transliteration: ___ den viastís, tha chásis to leoforío.

drills.translation: If you come early, we'll eat together.

drills.sentence: Αν {{answer}} τα χρήματα, θα αγοράσω το αυτοκίνητο.
drills.translation: If I have the money, I'll buy the car.
drills.transliteration: An ___ ta chrímata, tha agoráso to aftokínito.

points.title: The passive aorist (πλύθηκα, γράφτηκε)
points.function: Say a completed action was done to the subject
points.explanation:
The mediopassive has its own past: the passive aorist, endings -ηκα, -ηκες, -ηκε, -ήκαμε, -ήκατε, -ηκαν on a passive stem — πλένομαι → πλύθηκα 'I was washed', γράφομαι → γράφτηκε 'it was written', χάνομαι → χάθηκε 'it was lost'.

It reports one completed passive event.

references.title: Portal for the Greek Language (Centre for the Greek Language)

drills.translation: The letter was written in Greek.
The keys got lost last night.
The house was built a hundred years ago.
I was very glad when I heard the news.

drills.sentence: Η συνάντηση {{answer}} για την επόμενη εβδομάδα.
drills.translation: The meeting was scheduled for next week.
drills.transliteration: I sinándisi ___ yia tin epómeni evdomáda.

drills.sentence: Τα παιδιά {{answer}} γρήγορα πριν το σχολείο.
drills.translation: The children got ready quickly before school.
drills.transliteration: Ta pediá ___ grígora prin to scholío.

points.title: Unreal conditionals (αν + past, θα + past)
points.function: Talk about what would have happened
points.explanation:
For a hypothetical or counterfactual condition, αn + past tense pairs with θα + past in the result: αν είχα χρόνο, θα ερχόμουν 'if I had time, I would come/have come'; αν το ήξερα, θα σου το έλεγα 'if I had known, I'd have told you'.

Both clauses sit in the past; θα carries the 'would'.

references.title: Portal for the Greek Language (Centre for the Greek Language)

drills.sentence: Αν {{answer}} πλούσιος, θα ταξίδευα σε όλο τον κόσμο.
drills.translation: If I were rich, I would travel all over the world.
drills.transliteration: An ___ plúsios, tha taxídeva se ólo ton kósmo.

drills.sentence: Αν είχα χρόνο, {{answer}} ερχόμουν στο πάρτι.
drills.translation: If I had time, I would come to the party.
drills.transliteration: An ícha chróno, ___ erchómun sto párti.

drills.sentence: Αν το {{answer}}, θα σου το έλεγα αμέσως.
drills.translation: If I had known it, I'd have told you at once.
drills.transliteration: An to ___, tha su to élega amésos.

drills.sentence: Θα {{answer}} περισσότερο αν δεν ήταν τόσο αργά.
drills.translation: We would stay longer if it weren't so late.
drills.transliteration: Tha ___ perissótero an den ítan tóso argá.

drills.translation: If he had studied, he would pass easily.

drills.sentence: Αν μου το {{answer}}, θα σε βοηθούσα.
drills.translation: If you asked me, I would help you.
drills.transliteration: An mu to ___, tha se voithúsa.

points.title: Contracted verbs (αγαπάω / αγαπώ)
points.function: Conjugate the -άω/-ώ verb class
points.explanation:
A large group of verbs stresses the ending and has two interchangeable present forms, -άω and -ώ: αγαπάω/αγαπώ 'I love', μιλάω/μιλώ 'I speak', ρωτάω/ρωτώ 'I ask'.

The endings differ from plain -ω verbs: -άς, -άει/-ά, -άμε, -άτε, -άνε/-ούν.

Some (μπορώ, ζω) use only the -ώ set.

references.title: Portal for the Greek Language (Centre for the Greek Language)

drills.translation: I love Greek music very much.
You speak very fast, I don't understand you.

drills.sentence: Η δασκάλα {{answer}} τους μαθητές κάθε μέρα.
drills.translation: The teacher asks the students every day.
drills.transliteration: I daskála ___ tus mathités káthe méra.

drills.translation: We have been living in Athens for ten years.

drills.sentence: Τα παιδιά {{answer}} στην αυλή κάθε απόγευμα.
drills.translation: The children play in the yard every afternoon.
drills.transliteration: Ta pediá ___ stin avlí káthe apóyevma.

drills.translation: I can't come tonight, sorry.

points.title: One-word comparatives (καλύτερος, μεγαλύτερος)
points.function: Use the synthetic comparative and its irregulars
points.explanation:
Besides πιο, adjectives form a one-word comparative in -ότερος/-ύτερος and a superlative with the article: μεγάλος → μεγαλύτερος → ο μεγαλύτερος.

A few are irregular: καλός → καλύτερος 'better', κακός → χειρότερος 'worse', πολύς → περισσότερος 'more'.

references.title: Portal for the Greek Language (Centre for the Greek Language)

drills.sentence: Αυτό το εστιατόριο είναι {{answer}} από το άλλο.
drills.translation: This restaurant is better than the other one.
drills.transliteration: Aftó to estiatório íne ___ apó to állo.

drills.sentence: Ο καιρός σήμερα είναι {{answer}} από χθες.
drills.translation: The weather today is worse than yesterday.
drills.transliteration: O kerós símera íne ___ apó chthes.

drills.sentence: Η Αθήνα είναι η {{answer}} πόλη της Ελλάδας.
drills.translation: Athens is the biggest city in Greece.
drills.transliteration: I Athína íne i ___ póli tis Elládas.

drills.translation: We need more time for the project.

drills.sentence: Ο αδελφός μου είναι {{answer}} από μένα.
drills.translation: My brother is younger than me.
drills.transliteration: O adelfós mu íne ___ apó ména.

drills.translation: He is the best student in the class.

points.title: Participles (τρέχοντας, γραμμένος)
points.function: Add '…-ing' and '…-ed' descriptions
points.explanation:
The active participle in -οντας is invariable and means 'while …-ing': τρέχοντας 'running', χαμογελώντας 'smiling'.

The passive perfect participle in -μένος agrees like an adjective and means '…-ed / having been …': γραμμένος 'written', κουρασμένος 'tired', κλειστός → κλεισμένος 'closed'.

references.title: Portal for the Greek Language (Centre for the Greek Language)

drills.translation: He entered the room smiling.
Running for the bus, I fell.
The letter was written in Greek.
I was very tired after work.
I found the door locked.
He read the book lying on the couch.

points.title: Reported speech (είπε ότι / να)
points.function: Report what someone said or asked
points.explanation:
Report a statement with ότι/πως + a full clause: είπε ότι θα έρθει 'he said (that) he will come'. Report a command or request with να: μου είπε να περιμένω 'he told me to wait'.

Greek keeps the original tense — no backshift as in English.

references.title: Portal for the Greek Language (Centre for the Greek Language)

drills.translation: He told me that he'll be a bit late.

drills.sentence: Η δασκάλα είπε {{answer}} διαβάσουμε το κεφάλαιο.
drills.translation: The teacher said to read the chapter.
drills.transliteration: I daskála ípe ___ diavásume to kefáleo.

drills.translation: I think that he is right.

drills.sentence: Της ζήτησα {{answer}} μου τηλεφωνήσει το βράδυ.
drills.translation: I asked her to call me in the evening.
drills.transliteration: Tis zítisa ___ mu tilefonísi to vrádi.

drills.sentence: Είπαν {{answer}} το μαγαζί είναι κλειστό σήμερα.
drills.translation: They said that the shop is closed today.
drills.transliteration: Ípan ___ to magazí íne klistó símera.

drills.translation: He asked us whether we want coffee.

points.title: Pluperfect & future perfect (είχα γράψει, θα έχω γράψει)
points.function: Set one past event before another, or a future completion
points.explanation: The pluperfect είχα + aparémfato marks an action completed before another past point: είχα φύγει πριν έρθεις 'I had left before you came'. The future perfect θα έχω + aparémfato marks something that will be finished by a future point: θα έχω τελειώσει ως τότε 'I'll have finished by then'.

references.title: Portal for the Greek Language (Centre for the Greek Language)

drills.sentence: {{answer}} ήδη φάει όταν ήρθαν οι φίλοι μου.
drills.translation: I had already eaten when my friends came.
drills.transliteration: ___ ídi fái ótan írthan i fíli mu.

drills.translation: The train had left before we arrived.

drills.sentence: Ως το βράδυ θα {{answer}} τελειώσει την εργασία.
drills.translation: By evening I will have finished the assignment.
drills.transliteration: Os to vrádi tha ___ teliósi tin ergasía.

drills.sentence: Μέχρι τον Ιούνιο {{answer}} είχαμε μάθει πολλά.
drills.translation: By June we had already learned a lot.
drills.transliteration: Méchri ton Iúnio ___ íchame máthi pollá.

drills.sentence: Όταν γυρίσεις, θα {{answer}} καθαρίσει το σπίτι.
drills.translation: When you get back, I'll have cleaned the house.
drills.transliteration: Ótan yirísis, tha ___ katharísi to spíti.

drills.sentence: Δεν είχε {{answer}} ποτέ ελληνικά πριν έρθει εδώ.
drills.translation: He had never spoken Greek before he came here.
drills.transliteration: Den íche ___ poté elliniká prin érthi edó.

points.title: Deponent & irregular verbs (έρχομαι, γίνομαι)
points.function: Master common verbs with irregular stems
points.explanation:
Several everyday verbs are deponent (passive form, active meaning) and have suppletive aorists you must know: έρχομαι 'come' → ήρθα, γίνομαι 'become' → έγινα, κάθομαι 'sit' → κάθισα, φοβάμαι 'fear' → φοβήθηκα.

Their aorists don't follow the present stem at all.

references.title: Portal for the Greek Language (Centre for the Greek Language)

drills.translation: Yesterday I came home very late.
What did you become after school? — A teacher.

drills.sentence: Ο παππούς {{answer}} στην πολυθρόνα και κοιμήθηκε.
drills.translation: Grandpa sat in the armchair and fell asleep.
drills.transliteration: O pappús ___ stin polithróna ke kimíthike.

drills.sentence: Το παιδί {{answer}} τον σκύλο και έκλαψε.
drills.translation: The child was afraid of the dog and cried.
drills.transliteration: To pedí ___ ton skílo ke éklapse.

drills.translation: Last year many tourists came to the island.
Suddenly the sky became grey.

points.title: Subordinating connectors (επειδή, ενώ, αν και, ώστε)
points.function: Build complex sentences with cause, contrast, and result
points.explanation:
Written and formal Greek links clauses precisely: επειδή / διότι 'because', ενώ 'while/whereas', αν και / παρόλο που 'although', ώστε / έτσι ώστε 'so that / with the result that', εφόσον 'as long as / since'.

Each introduces a subordinate clause with its own subject and verb.

references.title: Portal for the Greek Language (Centre for the Greek Language)

drills.translation: I didn't come because I was ill.
While I was working, he was sleeping.

drills.sentence: {{answer}} και ήταν αργά, συνεχίσαμε τη συζήτηση.
drills.translation: Although it was late, we continued the conversation.
drills.transliteration: ___ ke ítan argá, sinechísame ti sizítisi.

drills.sentence: Μίλησε δυνατά, {{answer}} να τον ακούσουν όλοι.
drills.translation: He spoke loudly, so that everyone could hear him.
drills.transliteration: Mílise dinatá, ___ na ton akúsun óli.

drills.sentence: Θα σε βοηθήσω, {{answer}} μου το ζητήσεις ευγενικά.
drills.translation: I'll help you, as long as you ask me politely.
drills.transliteration: Tha se voithíso, ___ mu to zitísis evyeniká.

drills.translation: She stayed home because it was raining a lot.

points.title: Emphasis & clitic doubling (τον Γιάννη τον ξέρω)
points.function: Front a phrase for emphasis and double it with a pronoun
points.explanation:
Greek word order is flexible for emphasis.

A fronted (topicalized) object is often 'doubled' by a matching weak pronoun on the verb: τον Γιάννη τον ξέρω καλά 'John, I know him well', της Μαρίας της το είπα 'to Maria, I told her'.

This foregrounds the topic — very common in speech.

references.title: Portal for the Greek Language (Centre for the Greek Language)

drills.translation: John, I've known him since childhood.

drills.sentence: Το βιβλίο {{answer}} έχω διαβάσει δύο φορές.
drills.translation: The book, I've read it twice.
drills.transliteration: To vivlío ___ écho diavási dío forés.

drills.translation: To Maria, I told it to her yesterday.
The neighbours, I don't see them often.
The truth, everyone knows it.
It was you I was waiting for all evening.

points.title: Register & fixed expressions (formal vs everyday)
points.function: Shift between formal and colloquial Greek
points.explanation:
Modern Greek carries a learned layer (from Katharévousa) alongside everyday speech. Formal registers prefer words like δύναται 'is able', οφείλω 'I ought', εντούτοις 'nevertheless', καθότι 'since', while speech uses μπορεί, πρέπει, ωστόσο, γιατί.

Fixed phrases (εν πάση περιπτώσει 'in any case', κατά τη γνώμη μου 'in my opinion') mark tone.

references.title: Portal for the Greek Language (Centre for the Greek Language)

drills.translation: In any case, we'll talk tomorrow.
In my opinion, he is right.
It was hard; nevertheless, we managed.
I ought to admit that I was wrong.
He didn't come, since he was ill.
I'll do it in due course.
drills.hint: in (fixed phrase εν ευθέτω χρόνω / εν ώρα)

points.title: Demonstratives (αυτός ο…, εκείνος)
points.function: Point at things — this and that
points.explanation:
Greek demonstratives keep the article: αυτό ΤΟ βιβλίο (this book — literally 'this the book'). αυτός/αυτή/αυτό for near, εκείνος/εκείνη/εκείνο for far — both agree with the noun in gender, number, and case: αυτόν τον άντρα (accusative).

Without a noun they stand alone as pronouns: Θέλω αυτό. Προτιμώ εκείνα.

references.title: Portal for the Greek Language (Centre for the Greek Language)

related.title: Definite articles (ο / η / το)
related.contrast: The article never drops: αυτό το βιβλίο, never *αυτό βιβλίο.

drills.translation: This book is very good.

drills.sentence: {{answer}} ο άνθρωπος είναι ο γείτονάς μου.
drills.translation: This man is my neighbor.
drills.transliteration: ___ o ánthropos íne o gítonás mou.

drills.translation: This film was boring.

drills.sentence: {{answer}} το σπίτι εκεί πέρα είναι δικό μας.
drills.translation: That house over there is ours.
drills.transliteration: ___ to spíti ekí péra íne dikó mas.

drills.translation: That woman is a teacher.
I want these shoes.
